storm.summary_layperson: A line of thunderstorms is approaching from the west; Long Island Sound's cool water will take some edge off the storms, but with very high atmospheric energy today, organized cells could still push through with strong wind and heavy rain.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Sound water at 72°F vs. air at 87°F (15°F gap) creates a moderate marine layer that typically weakens incoming storms, though CAPE of 3,260 J/kg is high enough for stronger cells to punch through.
storm.detail_html:
A line of thunderstorm cells stretches 22–46 miles to the west of the cove, moving SE at 23 knots (radar-tracked from two KOKX scans). The lead cell sits 22 miles west at 38 dBZ — moderate intensity. Behind it, cells strengthen steadily to 47, 56, and 62 dBZ between 32 and 46 miles out. Deep-layer shear is very strong at 57 knots, supporting organized, long-lived convection. CAPE reads 3,260 J/kg — high instability — and the OKX forecast discussion specifically calls out the risk of strong gusty winds if any storms develop and strengthen.

The critical local modifier is Long Island Sound. Sound water is 72°F against inland air at 86–87°F — a 14°F gap that creates a moderate marine stable layer over the water. Storm cells approaching from the west must cross that layer before reaching the cove, which often chokes low-level updrafts, weakens storms, or pushes gust fronts ahead of the rain. This is what keeps Riverside's storm outcomes different from Westchester or White Plains on the same day.

However, CAPE at 3,260 J/kg is just above the 3,000 J/kg punch-through threshold — the point where organized storms can override the marine-layer resistance. The pipeline rates the Sound effect as moderate (not high), reflecting this tension. Most likely outcome: the cove sees wind and rain, but weaker than inland areas. The strongest 56–62 dBZ cells in the trailing portion of the line are most likely to push through if they hold together across the Sound.

The nearest cell (38 dBZ, 22 mi W) arrives in the 5:20–6:11 PM window — that's the gust front. Stronger cells follow: the 47 dBZ cell arrives around 6:15 PM, and the 56–62 dBZ cells (the heaviest portion) are on a 6:10–7:47 PM schedule with late edges stretched by the marine layer. When the marine layer is active, lean toward the late edge for rain/thunder arrival; the high CAPE pushes back toward the early edge. No precipitation has been recorded at any upstream APRS station yet — all rain counters show 0 mm — confirming the line hasn't crossed the coast.

What changes the call: if APRS stations to the WSW (N1TKS at Port Chester, GW2598 at Rye) report gusts jumping above 20 kt, the gust front is nearly here and arrival will likely beat the ETA. If cells weaken as they cross the Sound — watch for the 47–62 dBZ echoes shrinking between the Battery and the CT shore on live radar — expect a wind event without much lightning or heavy rain. A Heat Advisory runs through 9 PM and an Air Quality Alert (wildfire smoke) is also active.

arrival.blurb: Radar-tracked cell 22 mi W closing at 16 kt; the moderate marine layer may slow or weaken arrival, but high CAPE (3,260 J/kg) keeps punch-through risk elevated — lean toward the early edge of the window.

hourly.shift_note: steady NNW, storm closing from W
storm arrival window — HRRR may understate squall gusts

wind.tactics_blurb: NNW offshore flow gives gusty, puffy conditions in the cove — stay center for most consistent pressure, with big puffs off the NW shore. Storm line arrives 5:20–6:11 PM; conditions will deteriorate rapidly at that point and any races later in the window will be in significantly lighter and variable wind.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Wind regime is B-moderate (gradient-only). Sea breeze is completely absent — 0% of coastal stations reporting southerly flow — and the synoptic NNW gradient at 10–14 kt is running offshore, preventing any thermal from establishing. DeltaT is 13.7°F (87°F air vs 72°F Sound water), which would normally support a sea breeze, but the offshore gradient is overriding it entirely. HRRR projects NNW 332° at 12 kt at 17:00, trending to NW 321° and 5 kt by 21:00 as the post-frontal gradient relaxes.

N–NNW offshore flow in the cove produces the classic puffy, shifty pattern: big puffs off the NW shore from terrain channeling, holes between them, and best average pressure in the middle of the course away from the immediate lee of trees and buildings. There's no strong argument for either shore side in this direction — center start, center beat.

Shift character is oscillating puffs (15–30° swing) typical of offshore NNW flow, not a persistent one-way frontal shift. The cold front has already pushed through (generating the NNW gradient), so no major directional rotation is imminent — just normal gust/lull cycling. The wrinkle is the approaching storm line: the 5:20–6:11 PM arrival window could produce a brief squall burst with direction jumping 20–30° before normalizing. After storm passage, direction returns to NNW/NW and wind softens steadily through the evening.

HRRR/obs divergence: HRRR fh2 shows 332° NNW at 12 kt; RYC currently reads 9° N at 14.5 kt — a 37° divergence, with obs more northerly than the model. This is characteristic of post-frontal coastal flow where the model under-resolves near-surface wind backing toward the water. This is Regime B (not A/A+), so the maturation correction does not apply; use the HRRR direction trend (backing gradually from 332° to 315°) while treating the current RYC reading as the live cove truth. Direction confidence is rated moderate by the pipeline.

At 12 kt with gusts to 16 kt, conditions are powered-up keelboat territory — good racing for most classes, sporty for dinghies. The critical issue is storm arrival: the 5:20 PM early edge puts any race starting after ~16:45 EDT at risk of lightning or squall cancellation. Post-storm conditions (19:00–22:00) drop to 5–9 kt — still raceable but light and gusty. If the RC gets races in before the storm and restarts after, expect 7–9 kt NNW with big puffs and oscillating shifts.

data_caveats: KBDR anemometer down — coastal CT wind sensor missing from METAR
KHPN anemometer down — Westchester inland wind sensor missing
